text: With the rapid advancement of robotic surgery, there is a need to develop a robotic surgical education assessment and training tool similar to FLS for laparoscopic surgical training. The objective of this video is to develop and test an all-in-one psychomotor skills prototype for possible use in development of the fundamentals of robotic surgery curriculum.

The video displays a prototype device constructed from household materials, including a halved eight-inch styrofoam ball, felt material, foam shapes, wire, magnets, hair ties, puff paint, a shirt button, and a latex swimming cap.

The seven skills evaluated by this prototype include docking and instrument insertion, ender wrist manipulation, knot tying, suturing skills, utilization and switching of the fourth robotic arm, cutting and dissection, energy use and dissection.
The video shows a simulation setup with a dome prototype positioned inside a robotic docking model, illustrating the arrangement of components for robotic surgical training.
The video displays the bipolar Maryland dissector, monopolar curved scissors, needle driver, and other instruments used in the robotic simulation setup.
The learner docks the robotic system and inserts the instruments, ensuring all instruments are visible within the field of view of the simulation setup.
The second task is the ring tower transfer.

The learner uses one robotic arm to pick up a ring from the middle tower without touching the wire, transfers the ring to the other robotic arm, and places it on the side tower, ensuring not to touch the S-wire. This sequence is repeated twice.
The goal of this task is to evaluate end-to-wrist manipulation, hand-eye coordination, use of camera and clutch, and atraumatic candling of tissue.
The learner performs a surgeon's knot, ensuring the eyelets touch, followed by executing one throw using surgical instruments.
The learner performs horizontal mattress suturing through designated target points, demonstrating needle handling and suturing in various directions as part of the railroad track task.

In this example, an ovicle is used. As you can see here, a button was used to affix the beginning of the suture. In future models, the suture will be longer in order for the learner to finish the railroad track suturing with knot tying.

The learner uses one robotic arm to pick up the simulated vein while the other arm provides retraction. The learner then switches to the monopolar scissors with the fourth arm to cut the vein at the marked hash lines, demonstrating the use of multiple robotic arms and precision cutting with the monopolar scissors.

The learner performs a clover leaf dissection by cutting a pattern on the latex skin layer without penetrating the underlying colored felt material, demonstrating precise cutting and dissecting skills while carefully handling the tissue to avoid tearing.

This exercise also evaluates manipulation, cutting and dissection of tissue at various angles. Additionally, the exercise forces the learner to use multiple arms to allow for better exposure and retraction, and the learner must learn to maneuver tissue similar to situations encountered in the operating room.

The learner retracts the cut-out flap using the fourth arm, exposing the underlying tissue and vessel. The bipolar instrument is used to cauterize the vessel, followed by cutting with monopolar scissors, demonstrating the use of multiple arms and energy sources for dissection and cutting.

These next few pictures demonstrate the model after completion of all exercises. The total time for a novice to intermediate learner to complete all exercises was fifteen minutes. This all-in-one model is reusable and only the skin of the dome needs to be removed and replaced with a new skin for the next learner. Also, magnets were used to allow for easy placement and removal of the cones. The total cost for this model was approximately fifteen dollars.

Although some improvements and adjustments are to be applied to future models, this video demonstrates the feasibility of creating an all-in-one economic psychomotor skills model for robotic surgery training.
